back_translation: When morning came, the chief priests and all the elders of the people conferred together against Jesus in order to put Him to death.
So they bound Him, led Him away, and handed Him over to Pilate the governor.

At that time, when Judas, the one who had handed Him over, saw that Jesus had been condemned, he was filled with regret and brought the thirty pieces of silver back to return them to the chief priests and the elders,
and said, "I have sinned by handing over innocent blood to death." But they replied, "What is that matter to us? You yourself take care of it."
So he flung those pieces of silver into the temple and went out, then he went and hanged himself.
The chief priests picked up those pieces of silver and said, "It is not lawful by the Law to put this money into the treasury of the temple, because it is the price of blood."
So they conferred together, and used that money to buy a field of a potter as a place to bury foreigners.
For this reason, that field has been called the Field of Blood until this very day.

At that time was fulfilled what was spoken by Jeremiah the prophet, who said, "And they took the thirty pieces of silver, the price that the children of Israel had set for the one whom they had priced,
and they used that money to buy the potter's field, just as the Lord had commanded me."
Now Jesus stood before the governor, and the governor questioned Him, saying, "Are you the King of the Jews?" Jesus answered, "You yourself have said it."
And when the chief priests and the elders accused Him, He did not say anything in reply.
Then Pilate said to Him, "Do you not hear how many things they testify against you?"
He did not answer him even one word, so that the governor was greatly amazed.
Now during that festival, the governor had a custom of releasing one prisoner to the crowd as they desired.
At that time there was a notorious prisoner named Jesus Barabbas.
So when they had assembled, Pilate asked them, "Which one do you all want me to release to you, Jesus Barabbas, or Jesus who is called the Christ?"
For Pilate already knew that they had handed Him over because of envy.
While Pilate was sitting on the judgment seat, his wife sent someone to tell him, "Do not do anything with that righteous man, for today I have suffered greatly in a dream because of that man."
But the chief priests and the elders persuaded the crowds to ask for Barabbas and to destroy Jesus.
The governor answered and said to them, "Which of these two do you want me to release to you?" They replied, "Barabbas."
Pilate said to them, "What then shall I do with Jesus, who is called the Christ?" They all said, "Let him be crucified."
Pilate said, "Why? What evil has he done?" But they kept shouting all the louder, "Let him be crucified."

When Pilate saw that he could do nothing, but that a riot would break out instead, he took water and washed his hands before the crowd, and said, "I am not guilty of the blood of this man. You yourselves take care of it."
All the people answered, "Let his blood fall upon us and upon our descendants."
So Pilate released Barabbas to them, and after flogging Jesus, he handed Him over to be crucified.
Then the soldiers of the governor took Jesus to the headquarters Praetorium, and assembled the whole company of soldiers around Him.
They stripped off His clothes, and put a deep-red robe on Him.
They wove a crown of thorns and placed it on His head, and put a reed in His right hand. They knelt down before Him and mocked Him, saying, "Long live the King, King of the Jews!"
They spat on Him, and took the reed and struck His head over and over again.
When they had mocked Him, they took off that robe and put His own clothes on Him, and led Him out to crucify Him.
As they were going out, they met a certain man from Cyrene named Simon, and they conscripted that man to carry His cross.
When they came to a place named Golgotha, which means the Place of the Skull,
they gave Him wine mixed with gall to drink. When He had tasted it, He did not desire to drink it.
When they had crucified Him on the cross, they divided up His royal garments by drawing lots.
They sat there and kept watch over Him.
They posted the placard of the charge against Him written above His head, saying, "This is Jesus, King of the Jews."
At that time, two robbers were crucified together with Him, one on the right side and one on the left side.
And those who passed by reviled Him, shaking their heads,
and saying, "You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down from the cross."
Likewise, the chief priests, together with the scribes and the elders, mocked Him and said,
"He saved others, but he cannot save himself. He is the King of Israel, isn't he? Let him come down from the cross right now, and we will believe in him.
He trusts in God; let God help him now if He wants him. For he said, 'I am the Son of God.'"
The robbers who were crucified together with Him also reviled Him in the same way.
From noon until three in the afternoon, darkness covered all the land.
About three in the afternoon, Jesus cried out with a loud voice, "Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani," which means, "My God, my God, why have You forsaken me?"
When some of those standing there heard, they said, "This man is calling for Elijah."
Immediately one of them ran and took a sponge, soaked it in sour wine until it was saturated, then put it on the end of a reed and held it up to give Him to drink.
But the others who remained said, "Don't do that. Let us wait and see whether Elijah will come to save him."
Jesus cried out with a loud voice once again, then yielded up His spirit.
Behold, the curtain in the temple was torn in two from top to bottom. The earth quaked, and the rocks split open.
The tombs were opened, and the bodies of many saints who had fallen asleep were raised.
After He had risen, they came out of the tombs and entered the holy city, and appeared to many people.
When the centurion and those who were with him guarding Jesus saw the earthquake and the things that took place, they were exceedingly afraid and said, "Truly this man was the Son of God."
There were many women there watching from afar, who were those who had followed Jesus from the region of Galilee in order to serve Him.
Among them were Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joseph, and the mother of the sons of Zebedee.
When evening came, a certain rich man from the city of Arimathea named Joseph came; he himself was also a disciple of Jesus.
He went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. Pilate then ordered that the body be given to him.
Joseph received the body and wrapped it in clean linen cloth,
and laid the body in his own new tomb that had been hewn out in the rock; then he rolled a great stone to close the door of the tomb, and went away.
Mary Magdalene and another Mary were there, sitting opposite the tomb.
On the next day, which is the day after the day of Preparation, the chief priests and the Pharisees assembled before Pilate
and said, "Sir, we remember that while that deceiver was still alive, he said, 'After three days I will rise.'

Therefore command that the tomb be made secure until the third day, lest his disciples come and steal away the body and tell the people, 'He has risen from the dead,' and then this last deception will be even more grievous than the first."
Pilate said to them, "You have a guard unit. Go, secure the tomb as you know how."
So they went and made the tomb secure by sealing the stone and posting the guard unit.
